title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
5 Investment Lessons To Remember From The 2008 Financial Crisis
Money and finances are a concern for almost everyone. For someone who earns or spends money, or does both, it is important to remember what investment lessons and finance-handling tips time has taught us collectively. One such event that has left quite a mark on the consciousness anyone who deals with money, is the 2008 Financial Crisis.
Also known as the Global Financial Crisis, the 2008 recession affected nearly the entire world. It took people quite a while to get a grasp of the happenings and sort out their finances. While there were several reasons why the 2008 Financial Crisis occurred, right now, the key thing to remember is that it is behind us. The more important part to focus on is what it has taught us, and how we can implement these lessons.
Key Learnings from the Financial Crisis
Whether you are someone who invests their money or not, know that money lessons may come in handy anytime. Here are some of the investment lessons from the Global Financial Crisis that everyone should be aware of.
Patience is a virtue
During the 2008 recession, investors were starting to lose a significant value of their portfolios. For some of them, the next step seemed to be getting rid of the stocks that were losing value. However, there were some who decided to wait it out and not take any harsh decisions before analysing the situation. As it turns out, even the stocks that were losing value were fundamentally strong. Thus, it was the right call to wait around for them to bounce back. What is important to remember from this incident is that when taking portfolio decisions, patience is a virtue.
Diversifying your portfolio is advisable
Traditionally, stocks from a few sectors are considered relatively safer than others. This usually includes stocks from financial service providers, banks, and other such institutions. This meant that investors who tried to lean towards safety leaned heavily on stocks from these sectors. However, during the financial crisis of 2008, these types of investments suffered extensively. What it taught investors is that irrespective of how safe or how lucrative a sector may seem, it is not ideal to keep all your eggs in a single basket. Even today, one of the common tips you may come across when making an investment plan, is to take the time and effort to diversify your portfolio. If you are a beginner and looking to play it even safer, you can try plans like ULIPs, which are largely managed by fund managers.
Avoid timing the market
Timing the market is one of the oldest tricks in the book, but that does not make it worthwhile. However, know that it is not really possible to predict the highs and lows of the market and create your investment portfolio based on that. Trying to time the market may end up doing you more damage than profits. Instead, depend on a sound investment plan that takes into account your goals and resources to help you map a suitable investment path. You can either take the help of an investment advisor or get started with an investment calculator to see if you are taking the right step next in your investment journey.
Make informed decisions
It may be tempting, but it may not help to blindly follow the trends when it comes to investments. Instead, having an investment plan and following it through, may help. Take the time to understand the ups and downs of the market, what causes them, and what affects them. Also, understand which types of investments would suit you and why. Stay actively involved with your investments and be conscious about where you are directing your money.
Don’t get lured by quick hacks
Many people lose patience and want to try quick and easy ways to make money. They tend to abandon their investment plans in favour of simpler tricks that offer faster ways to more money. However, such ways may lead to losing more money than what was gained. It is better to stick to a plan and take slow yet well-calculated, steady steps. If you are only starting on your investment journey, you can start with an investment calculator to help you make a plan.
Besides focusing on investment and multiplying your wealth, also pay some attention to keeping your family secure. You can use life insurance to do the same. Plans like ULIPs can give you dual benefits of insurance and wealth creation. However, if you want to keep your insurance and investments separate, you can opt for a term life insurance plan for your life cover.
Events like the Financial Crisis of 2008 happen once in a long while. But they end up reminding you of what is important and what are some of the to deal with your money. These learnings from the previous crisis should not only help you sail through another crisis if it were to happen, but also help you with your overall investment journey.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article is not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. full_pdf_text:
3 Investment Lessons To Remember From The 2008 Financial
Crisis
Money and finances may be a concern for some people. For someone who earns or spends money, or does both, it is important to remember what investment lessons and finance-handling tips time has taught us collectively. One such event that may have left quite a mark on the consciousness of anyone who deals with money, is the 2008 Financial Crisis1.
Also colloquially known as the Global Financial Crisis, the 2008 recession1 has probably affected nearly the entire world. People might have taken quite a while to get a grasp of the happenings and sort out their finances. While there may have been several reasons why the 2008 Financial Crisis occurred, right now, the key thing to remember is that it is behind us. The more important part to focus on is what it has taught us, and how we can implement these lessons.
Key Learnings from the Financial Crisis
Whether you are someone who invests their money or not, know that money lessons may come in handy anytime. Here are some of the investment lessons from the Global Financial Crisis that everyone should be aware of.
 Timing the market
Timing the market is one of the oldest tricks in the book, but that may not make it worthwhile. However, know that it is not really possible to predict the highs and lows of the market and create your investment portfolio based on that. Trying to time the market always may not be practical and may end up causing you more damage than profits. Instead, choose a suitable investment plan taking into account your goals and resources to help you map a suitable investment path. You can either take the help of an investment advisor or get started with an investment calculator to see if you are taking the required step next in your investment journey.
 Make informed decisions
It may be tempting, but it may not be helpful to blindly follow the trends when it comes to investments. Instead, having an investment plan and following it through, may help. Consider taking time to understand the ups and downs of the market, what causes them, and what affects them. Also, consider understanding which types of investments would suit you and why. You may stay actively involved with your investments and be conscious about where you are directing your money.
 Don’t get lured by quick hacks
Many people may lose patience and want to try quick and easy ways to make money. They might abandon their investment plans in favour of simpler tricks that offer faster ways of making more money. However, such ways may lead to losing more money than what was gained. It is preferred to stick to a plan and take slow yet well-calculated, steady steps. If you are only starting on your investment journey, you can start with an investment calculator to help you make a plan.
Besides focusing on investment and multiplying your wealth, also consider paying some attention to keeping your family financially secure in your absence. You may consider using life insurance to do the same. Plans like ULIPs can give you dual benefits of insurance and market-linked wealth creation. However, if you want to keep your insurance and investments separate, you may opt for a term life insurance plan for your life cover.
Events like the Financial Crisis of 2008 happen once in a long while. But they end up reminding you of what is important and what are some of the ways to deal with your money. These learnings from the previous crisis not only help you sail through another crisis if it were to happen, but also might help you with your overall investment journey.

The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions.
